q: In a metre-bridge when a resistance in the left gap is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and unknown resistance in the right gap, the balance length is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> On shunting the unknown resistance with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the balance length changes by:
question: In a metre-bridge when a resistance in the left gap is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and unknown resistance in the right gap, the balance length is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> On shunting the unknown resistance with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the balance length changes by:
solution:
In first circuit:

For balanced condition, we get 240=X60⇒X=3Ω

In second case:

Net resistance of the right side due to shunting becomes,  X'=2×32+3=1.2 Ω

Again for balanced condition, 

2ℓ=1.2100-ℓ

⇒200-2ℓ=1.2ℓ

⇒ℓ=2003.2=62.5 cm

Clearly, balance length changes by 22.5 cm.

subject: Physics
q: The drift velocity of electrons for a conductor connected in an electrical circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>d</mi></msub></math>. The conductor is now replaced by another conductor with same material and same length but double the area of cross-section. The applied voltage remains same. The new drift velocity of electrons will be
question: The drift velocity of electrons for a conductor connected in an electrical circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>d</mi></msub></math>. The conductor is now replaced by another conductor with same material and same length but double the area of cross-section. The applied voltage remains same. The new drift velocity of electrons will be
solution:
Drift velocity of electrons through a conductor kept in an external electric field is given by Vd=-eEmτ=-eVmlτ 

Where, e=magnitude of charge on electron, m=mass of electron, E=external electric field and τ=average relaxation time.

As, drift velocity is independent of area. So, the new drift velocity of electrons will be Vd.

subject: Physics
q: A rectangular parallelopiped is measured as 1 cm×1 cm×100 cm. If its specific resistance is 3×10-7Ω m, then the resistance between its two opposite rectangular faces will be _____×10-7Ω.
question: A rectangular parallelopiped is measured as 1 cm×1 cm×100 cm. If its specific resistance is 3×10-7Ω m, then the resistance between its two opposite rectangular faces will be _____×10-7Ω.
solution:
It is given that 

l = 1 cm = 0.01 m

A = 100 cm × 1 cm= 100 cm2

⇒A= 0.01 m2

ρ=3×10-7 Ω m

Using the formula of resistance,

R=ρlA=3×10-7×0.010.01=3×10-7Ω

subject: Physics
q: A potentiometer wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is connected in series with a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> battery and an external resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. A cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> in secondary circuit is balanced by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> long potentiometer wire. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> (in volt) is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
question: A potentiometer wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is connected in series with a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> battery and an external resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. A cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> in secondary circuit is balanced by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> long potentiometer wire. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> (in volt) is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
Since the given resistance is in series, so, total resistance R=20+30=50 Ω

Given,

Potential difference=25 V

Current I=VR=2550=0.5 A

Also, Potential gradient =VpdL=IRL

Where, Vpd=potential drop at wire

L=Length of wire

Potential gradient=20×0.510=1 V m-1

So, Emf of the cell is

E=Potential gradient×Balancing Length

E= 1× 250100

E= 2.5 V=2510 V

Thus, x=25.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A: Alloys such as constantan and manganin are used in making standard resistance coils.

Reason R: Constantan and manganin have very small value of temperature coefficient of resistance.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A: Alloys such as constantan and manganin are used in making standard resistance coils.

Reason R: Constantan and manganin have very small value of temperature coefficient of resistance.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A.
A is true but R is false.
A is false but R is true.
solution:
The alloys manganin and constantan are used for making standard resistance coils as they have high resistivity, low-temperature coefficient of resistance, ability to attain good resistance values in small size. Their resistance value would change very little with temperature.

Thus, both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

subject: Physics
q: The Wheatstone bridge shown in the figure below, gets balanced when the carbon resistor used as R1 has the colour code (orange, red, brown). The resistors R2 and R4 are 80 Ω and 40 Ω, respectively. Assuming that the colour code for the carbon resistors gives their accurate values, the colour code for the carbon resistor, used as R3, would be
question: The Wheatstone bridge shown in the figure below, gets balanced when the carbon resistor used as R1 has the colour code (orange, red, brown). The resistors R2 and R4 are 80 Ω and 40 Ω, respectively. Assuming that the colour code for the carbon resistors gives their accurate values, the colour code for the carbon resistor, used as R3, would be
solution:
Colour band A and B indicate the first two significant figures of resistance in 'ohm', while the C band gives the decimal multiplier i.e., the number of zeros that follows the two significant figures A and B.

The last band D indicates the tolerance in percentage about the indicated value or in other word, it represents the percentage accuracy of the indicated value. 

For R1, the given colour of the rings of a carbon resistor are orange, red, brown then, the value of resistance is

R1=32×101

Condition for balance in the Wheat stone's bridge is

R1R3=R2R4

So, 320R3=8040

∴R3=160 Ω, so colour coding of resistor R3 is

Brown→1

Blue→6

Brown→1
16×101⇒160 Ω

question:

solution:
Jc=Eρ=Vρd

Jd=1 Adqdt=CAdVcdt=ϵddVcdt

⇒V0sin2πftρd=10x×80ε0 d V0(2πf)cos2πft

tan2π×900800=10x×409×109×900

=x=6

q: In an electric circuit, a call of certain emf provides a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> across a load resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math> However, it provides a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> across a load resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math>The emf of the cell is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><menclose notation="bottom"><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo></menclose></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>.</mo></math>
question: In an electric circuit, a call of certain emf provides a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> across a load resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math> However, it provides a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> across a load resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math>The emf of the cell is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><menclose notation="bottom"><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo></menclose></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Terminal voltage, v=iR=ERR+r

1st→1.25=E(5)5+r  …i
2nd→1=E(2)2+r  …ii

By i and ii,
r=1 Ω, E=32 V=1510 volt
⇒x=15

subject: Physics
q: Which of the four resistances $P, Q, R$ and $S$ generate the greatest amount of heat when a current flows from A to B ?
question: Which of the four resistances $P, Q, R$ and $S$ generate the greatest amount of heat when a current flows from A to B ?
solution: $$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{R}_1=\mathrm{P}+\mathrm{Q}=2 \Omega+4 \Omega=6 \Omega \\ & \mathrm{R}_2=\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{S}=1 \Omega+2 \Omega=3 \Omega \\ & \mathrm{I}_1 \mathrm{R}_1=\mathrm{I}_2 \mathrm{R}_2 \\ & \mathrm{I}_1=\frac{\mathrm{R}_2}{\mathrm{R}_1} \mathrm{I}_2=\frac{3}{6} \mathrm{I}_2=\frac{\mathrm{I}_2}{2} \end{aligned} $$ or $\mathrm{I}_2=2 \mathrm{I}_1$ Heat flow $\mathrm{H}=\mathrm{I}^2 \mathrm{Rt}$ For $\mathrm{Q}, \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{Q}}=\mathrm{I}_1^2 \mathrm{Qt}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_2^2}{4} \times 4 \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{I}_2^2 \mathrm{t}$ For $\mathrm{S}, \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{S}}=\mathrm{I}_2^2 \mathrm{St}=\mathrm{I}_2^2 \cdot 2 \mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{I}_2^2 \mathrm{t}$ $\therefore$ Greatest amount of heat generated by $\mathrm{S}$.

When it is connected to a supply of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, a steady current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> passes in it and temperature is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><msup><mo> </mo><mi>o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> more than the room temperature. The temperature coefficient of resistance of the heating element is
question: A heating element has a resistance of <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> at room temperature. When it is connected to a supply of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, a steady current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> passes in it and temperature is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><msup><mo> </mo><mi>o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> more than the room temperature. The temperature coefficient of resistance of the heating element is
solution:

α=10100 ×500⇒α=2 ×10-4 °C-1

subject: Physics
q: A galvanmeter has a coil of resistance $200 \Omega$ with a full scale deflection at $20 \mu \mathrm{A}$. The value of resistance to be added to use it as an ammeter of range $(0-20) \mathrm{mA}$ is ;
question: A galvanmeter has a coil of resistance $200 \Omega$ with a full scale deflection at $20 \mu \mathrm{A}$. The value of resistance to be added to use it as an ammeter of range $(0-20) \mathrm{mA}$ is ;
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{G}=200 \Omega \\ & \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{g}}=20 \mu \mathrm{A} \\ & \mathrm{i}=\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{g}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{G}}{\mathrm{S}}+1\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 20 \times 10^{-3}=20 \times 10^{-6}\left(\frac{200}{\mathrm{~S}}+1\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{200}{\mathrm{~S}}=999 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{S} \approx 0.2 \Omega\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Refer to the figure given below. The values of $I_1$, $I_2$ and $I_3$ are __________.
question: Refer to the figure given below. The values of $I_1$, $I_2$ and $I_3$ are __________.
solution:
Using the principle of superposition, we analyse the circuit with one source active at a time.

Case 1: Only the $10\text{ V}$ source is active (the $5\text{ V}$ source is replaced by a short circuit).

With the $5\text{ V}$ source shorted, the top and bottom nodes of the bridge are directly connected by a wire, forming a Wheatstone bridge.

Checking for balance:
Left arm ratio: $\dfrac{4}{4} = 1$
Right arm ratio: $\dfrac{2}{2} = 1$

The ratios are equal, so the bridge is balanced. Hence, no current flows through the middle branch connecting the two nodes, and the contribution to $I_2$ from the $10\text{ V}$ source is $0\text{ A}$.

Equivalent resistance of the balanced bridge:
$(4 + 2)\,\Omega \parallel (4 + 2)\,\Omega = 6\,\Omega \parallel 6\,\Omega = 3\,\Omega$

Total resistance including the series $1\,\Omega$ resistor:
$R_{eq} = 3 + 1 = 4\,\Omega$

Current drawn from the $10\text{ V}$ source:
$I_{1(10V)} = \dfrac{10}{4} = 2.5\text{ A}$

This current splits equally between the two $6\,\Omega$ branches, so the current through the bottom $4\,\Omega$ resistor is:
$I_{3(10V)} = \dfrac{2.5}{2} = 1.25\text{ A}$

Case 2: Only the $5\text{ V}$ source is active (the $10\text{ V}$ source is replaced by a short circuit).

Now the $5\text{ V}$ source is applied across the top and bottom nodes, and the $1\,\Omega$ resistor lies in the middle branch between the left and centre nodes.

For the left branch ($4\,\Omega$ and $4\,\Omega$ in series), the potential at the midpoint is $\dfrac{5}{2} = 2.5\text{ V}$.
For the right branch ($2\,\Omega$ and $2\,\Omega$ in series), the potential at the midpoint is also $\dfrac{5}{2} = 2.5\text{ V}$.

Since both midpoints are at the same potential, no current flows through the $1\,\Omega$ middle branch. Hence, the contribution to $I_1$ from the $5\text{ V}$ source is $0\text{ A}$.

Equivalent resistance seen by the $5\text{ V}$ source:
$(4 + 4)\,\Omega \parallel (2 + 2)\,\Omega = 8\,\Omega \parallel 4\,\Omega = \dfrac{8 \times 4}{8 + 4} = \dfrac{8}{3}\,\Omega$

Total current supplied by the $5\text{ V}$ source:
$I_{2(5V)} = \dfrac{5}{8/3} = \dfrac{15}{8} = 1.875\text{ A}$

Current through the left branch (which includes the bottom $4\,\Omega$ resistor):
$I_{3(5V)} = \dfrac{5}{8} = 0.625\text{ A}$

Applying superposition:
$I_1 = I_{1(10V)} + I_{1(5V)} = 2.5 + 0 = 2.5\text{ A}$
$I_2 = I_{2(10V)} + I_{2(5V)} = 0 + 1.875 = 1.875\text{ A}$
$I_3 = I_{3(10V)} + I_{3(5V)} = 1.25 + 0.625 = 1.875\text{ A}$

Hence, the correct option is $(1)\ I_1 = 2.5\text{ A},\ I_2 = 1.875\text{ A},\ I_3 = 1.875\text{ A}$.

subject: Physics
q: The galvanometer deflection, when key K1 is closed but K2 is open, equals θ0 (see figure). On closing K2 also and adjusting R2 to 5Ω, the deflection in galvanometer becomes θ05. The resistance of the galvanometer is, then, given by [Neglect the internal resistance of battery]:
question: The galvanometer deflection, when key K1 is closed but K2 is open, equals θ0 (see figure). On closing K2 also and adjusting R2 to 5Ω, the deflection in galvanometer becomes θ05. The resistance of the galvanometer is, then, given by [Neglect the internal resistance of battery]:
solution:
θ​0∝iG⇒θ​0C=iG

subject: Physics
q: The value of current i1 flowing from A to C in the circuit diagram is :
question: The value of current i1 flowing from A to C in the circuit diagram is :
solution:
The Diagram shown in the question represents the balanced wheatstone bridge. It satisfy the condition of balanced wheat stone bridge.
Multiplication of dimensionally opposite resistance is same.
2×2=2×2⇒4=4
When the circuit is balanced wheat stone bridge then current through the branch BD is zero, so it can be removed.

Now, after removing BD wire voltage across AC is 8 V.

So, current will be,

i2=84+4=1 A

subject: Physics
q: A cylindrical wire of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and conductivity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is subjected to an electric field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mV</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The expected value of current in the wire will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>π</mi><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _________.
question: A cylindrical wire of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and conductivity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is subjected to an electric field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mV</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The expected value of current in the wire will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>π</mi><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _________.
solution:
Conductivity σ=5×107 S m-1

Radius r=0.5 mm=5×10-4 m

E=10×10-3 V m-1

J=σE=10×10-3×5×107

⇒J=5×105

⇒iA=5×105

i=5×105×πr2

=5×105×π×5×10-42

=125π×10-3 A

Therefore, i=125 π mA

Hence, x=5

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following statements is false?
question: Which of the following statements is false?
options: Kirchhoff's second law represents energy conservation
Wheatstone bridge is the most sensitive when all the four resistances are of the same order of magnitude
In a balanced Wheatstone bridge if the cell and the galvanometer are exchanged, the null point is disturbed
A rheostat can be used as a potential divider
solution:
Let us assume R1, R2, R3 & R4 is connected balanced wheat stone bridge, such that R1R3=R2R4  ...1 as shown below,

 
                                                                   
If galvanometer and cell is exchanged, then circuit becomes as shown in below figure, 


                                                                        

The circuit is redrawn and can be visualised as below figure, 



                                                                     
In this case, if it is balanced, then there must be, 
R1R2=R3R4  ...2

Equation 1 & 2 realises that, both equations are same, which means null point is not affected.

subject: Physics
q: In the given circuit, the equivalent resistance between the terminal A and B is ____ Ω
question: In the given circuit, the equivalent resistance between the terminal A and B is ____ Ω
solution:
In the given circuit, both 4 Ω resistors get shorted out, thus no current will flow from them. Removing the resistors from which no current will flow.

Now, the circuit can be redrawn as shown below.

Req=3+(2‖2)+6

⇒Req=3+2×22+2+6

⇒Req=10 Ω

subject: Physics
q: In the given circuit, the current flowing through the resistance 20 Ω is 0.3 A, while the ammeter reads 0.9 A. The value of R1 is _____Ω.
question: In the given circuit, the current flowing through the resistance 20 Ω is 0.3 A, while the ammeter reads 0.9 A. The value of R1 is _____Ω.
solution:
Given, i1=0.3 A, i1+i2+i3=0.9 A

So, VAB=i1×20 Ω=0.3×20 V=6 V

i2=6 V15Ω=25 A

Now,

i1+i2+i3=910 A

⇒310+25+i3=910

⇒710+i3=910

⇒i3=0.2 A

So, i3×R1=6 V

⇒(0.2)R1=6

⇒R1=60.2=30 Ω

subject: Physics
q: The combination of two identical cells, whether connected in series or parallel combination provides the same current through an external resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The value of internal resistance of each cell is
question: The combination of two identical cells, whether connected in series or parallel combination provides the same current through an external resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The value of internal resistance of each cell is
solution:
When the cells are connected in series is=2ε2r+R=2ε2r+2

When they are connected in parallel, the emf is εp=εr+εr1r+1r=ε and the effective internal resistance will be rp=r×rr+r=r2. Therefore, the current will be,

ip=εR+r2=ε2+r2=2ε4+r

Since, is=ip⇒2ε2r+2=2ε4+r⇒r=2 Ω

q: A cell, shunted by a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistance, is balanced across a potentiometer wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The balancing length is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> when the cell is shunted by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistance. The value of internal resistance of the cell will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A cell, shunted by a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistance, is balanced across a potentiometer wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The balancing length is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> when the cell is shunted by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistance. The value of internal resistance of the cell will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Potential difference across test battery is, ∵ V=ERR+r∝l; RR+r∝l

Therefore, we can write,  88+r44+r=32

⇒44+r=38+r  ⇒16+4r=24+3r  ⇒r=8 Ω
